next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 15
The laundry corridor under the casino sounded like weather trapped in a pipe. At one-forty in the morning, the washers banged into high spin and the ductwork answered with a deep metallic moan that ran the length of the room. Eli kept his left hand pressed to the warm steel of Dryer Three while he read the marker notes on the load card clipped above it. The card listed king sheets, forty-two pounds, and no bleach. He had learned to touch machines first and paperwork second, because the heat told him more than the card ever did.

His rib caught when he bent for the lint trap. The old crack on his left side did not stab anymore, but it still gripped him if he twisted wrong, a hard wire under the skin that yanked his breath short. He waited it out, one palm on his thigh, then stood and shut the trap with his forearm. Keep earning. Keep moving. Keep his head clear enough to send money east on Friday and another piece on Tuesday. If he treated every shift like numbers on a page, he could stay in front of shame for another week.

Mateo Rivas rolled a linen cart past him and whistled the same four bars from a norteno song he had never finished. He always stopped before the melody turned sad. The glitter bracelet on his wrist flashed in the fluorescent light, cheap pink plastic beads with SOFIA spelled in silver letters. His daughter had made it at school, he had said on Eli's first night, and he wore it even when the floor supervisor called it unsanitary and told him to cut it off.

"You're stacking those too high," Mateo said, nodding at Eli's cart. "You hit that doo...
open_hooks_to_carry: Hiring scene where desperation is visible in what Eli accepts
Work-floor details emphasize heat, noise, and repetitive strain
prev_tail_excerpt:
...and sent people down the ramp in a steady rhythm. No speeches, no hurry tone, just efficient hand signals and eye contact. As Eli reached the scanner, Sandeep glanced at his face and asked quietly, "You ate?"

"Enough," Eli said.

Sandeep nodded once, like that was the answer he expected from men in Eli's condition. "Seat 18A, left side. Outlet is loose; hold the plug up with your shoe."

The bus smelled like vinyl, old coffee, and whatever cleaner they used between runs when they had time to use cleaner at all. Eli stowed the duffel overhead and took the window seat. The glass was cold enough to sting when he leaned his temple against it.

Outside, the terminal lights smeared across the pane in yellow streaks. The security guard with gray braids stood near the curb reading the same western under a sodium lamp, one finger marking her page while she scanned the loading lane. She looked up as the bus pulled out and gave a small two-finger salute to nobody in particular.

Eli took out the wire receipt, unfolded it, and read Nora's name printed in block capitals above the amount. One hundred fifty dollars. Too small to fix anything. Large enough to remove one excuse.

The bus merged onto the freeway. Tire noise settled into a steady hum, low and relentless, like a machine set to keep counting whether he watched it or not. He slid the receipt back into the folder, zipped the folder shut, and looped the strap around his forearm the way the city driver had told him.

He did not sleep. He watched Utah thin into black desert and kept his hand on the folder until his fingers cramped.
state_deltas_required: Increase earnings in Reno fast enough to matter.
Eli commits to double shifts that worsen his rib injury but build a realistic repayment fund.
